concepts.title: Condition of No Emergence
concepts.conceptBody:
Condition of No Emergence 

The light will not emerge out of a prism for all values of angle of incidence if at face $AB$ for ${{i}_{1}}$ $=\text{max}=90$, at face $\text{AC}$

${{\text{r}}_{2}}>{{\theta }_{\text{C}}}$ …(1)

Now from Snell's law at face $AB$, we have

$1 \text{sin }90 =\mu \text{sin} {{r}_{1}}$

i.e. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{r}_{1}}=\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( 1/\mu \right)$

 

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{r}}_{1}}={{\theta }_{\text{C}}}$ …(2)

So from Eqs. (1) and (2)

${{\text{r}}_{1}}+{{\text{r}}_{2}}>2{{\theta }_{\text{C}}}$ …(3)

However, in a prism

${{r}_{1}}+{{r}_{2}}={{A}^{\text{*}}}$ …(4)

So from Eqs. (3) and (4)

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }>2{{\theta }_{C}}$ …(5)

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ sin}\left( \text{A}/2 \right)>\text{sin}{{\theta }_{\text{C}}}$

i.e. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\mu >\left[ \text{cosec}\left( \text{A}/2 \right) \right]$ ..(6)

i.e., a ray of light will not emerge out of a prism (whatever be the angle of incidence) if A > $2{{\theta }_{\text{C}}}$, i.e. if $\mu >\text{cosec}\left( \text{A}/2 \right)$.

concepts.title: Condition of Grazing Emergence
concepts.conceptBody:
Condition of Grazing Emergence

If a ray can emerge out of a prism, the value of angle of incidence ${{i}_{1}}$ for which angle of emergence ${{\text{i}}_{2}}=90$ is called condition of grazing emergence. In this situations as the ray emerges out of face AC, i.e. TIR does not take place at it,

 

${{\text{r}}_{2}}<{{\theta }_{\text{C}}}$ ….(1)

But as in a prism

$\begin{align} & {{\mathbf{r}}_{1}}+{{\mathbf{r}}_{2}}=\text{A} \\ & So {{\mathbf{r}}_{1}}=\text{A}-\left( <{{\theta }_{\text{C}}} \right) \\ & i.e., {{\mathbf{r}}_{1}}>\text{A}-{{\theta }_{\text{C}}} ...(2) \\ \end{align}$

Now from Snell’s law at face $AB$, we have

$1 \text{sin }{{i}_{1}}=\mu \text{sin }{{r}_{1}}$

which in the light of Eq. (2) gives

$\quad \quad \sin i_1>\mu \sin \left(A-\theta_C\right)$

$i.e.,\quad \quad \sin i_1>\mu\left[\sin A \cos \theta_C-\cos A \sin \theta_C\right]$

$i.e.,\quad \quad \sin i_1>\mu\left[(\sin A) \sqrt{\left(1-\sin ^2 \theta_C\right)}-\cos A \sin \theta_C\right]$

$i.e.,\quad \quad \sin i_1>\left[\sqrt{\left(\mu^2-1\right)} \sin A-\cos \mathrm{A}\right] \quad\left[\text { as } \sin \theta_C=(1 / \mu)\right]$

$or \quad \quad i_1>\sin ^{-1}\left[\sqrt{\left(\mu^2-1\right)} \sin \mathrm{A}-\cos \mathrm{A}\right]$

$or \quad \quad \left(i_1\right)_{\min }=\sin ^{-1}\left[\sqrt{\left(\mu^2-1\right)} \sin \mathrm{A}-\cos \mathrm{A}\right]$

i.e., light will emerge out of a prism only if angle of incidence is greater than ${{\left( {{i}_{1}} \right)}_{\text{min }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ given by Eq. (3). In this situation deviation will be given by $\delta =\left( {{i}_{1}}+90-A \right)$ with ${{i}_{1}}$ given by Eq. (3).

concepts.title: Important Points of Prism
concepts.conceptBody:
Important Points of Prism

The deviation produced by a prism depends on angle of incidence, ${{i}_{1}}$, angle of prism A, $\mu $ of the material of the prism and wavelength of light $\lambda $. So

1.For a given A, material of prism and light, as angle of incidence increases, angle of deviation first decreases, reaches a minimum and then increases, as shown in Fig. (A).

 

                          (A)

2.For a given material of prism, light and angle of incidence, as angle of prism increases, angle of deviation also increases,

i.e. $\delta \propto \mathrm{A}$

e.g., deviation in 60 prism will be more than in 30 prism.

3.For a given angle of incidence, angle of prism and light, as material of prism is changed, deviation will also change. Greater the $\mu $ or the prism, greater will be the deviation as,

$\delta \propto \left( \mu -1 \right)$

e.g., $\mu $ of flint glass is more than that of crown glass, so ${{\delta }_{F}}>{{\delta }_{C}}$, for same ${{i}_{1}},A$ and $\lambda $.

4.For a given angle of incidence, angle of prism and material of prism, if wavelength of light is changed, deviation will also change as $\mu $ of a prism is different for different wavelengths. With increases in wavelength deviation decreases, i.e., deviation for red is least while maximum for violet [Fig. (B)]. (This is discussed in detail in the next section).

concepts.title: Dispersion of Light
concepts.conceptBody:
Dispersion of Light

When white light passes through a prism it splits up into constituent colors. This phenomenon is called dispersion and arises due to the fact that refractive index of a prism is different for different wavelengths. So different wavelengths in passing through a prism are deviated through different angles and as $\delta \propto \left( \mu -1 \right)$, violet is deviated most while red least giving rise to display of colors known as spectrum [Fig. (B)]. Rainbow, the most colorful phenomenon in nature, is primarily due to the dispersion of sunlight by rain-drops suspended in air.

In case of dispersion of light the angle between the extreme rays is a measure of dispersion and is called angular dispersion. So

Angular dispersion $\theta =\left( {{\delta }_{\text{V}}}-{{\delta }_{\text{R}}} \right)$ …(13)

and the ratio of dispersion to mean deviation is called dispersive power $\omega $ i.e.,

$\omega =\frac{\theta }{\delta }=\frac{{{\delta }_{\text{V}}}-{{\delta }_{\text{R}}}}{\delta }$ …(14)

But as for a prism $\delta=(\mu-1) \mathrm{A}$,

$\omega =\frac{{{\mu }_{\text{V}}}-{{\mu }_{\text{R}}}}{(\mu -1)}=\frac{\text{d}\mu }{(\mu -1)}\quad \left\{ \mu ={{\mu }_{\text{Y}}}=\frac{\left( {{\mu }_{\text{V}}}+{{\mu }_{\text{R}}} \right)}{2} \right\}\ldots (15)$

From expressions (14) and (15) it is clear that :

1.Dispersive power like refractive index has no units and dimensions and depends on the material of the prism.

2.As for a given prism dispersive power is constant,

$\theta \propto \delta $

i.e., dispersion of different wavelengths will be different and will be maximum for violet and minimum for red

(as deviation is maximum for violet and minimum for red), i.e., in the spectrum of white light produced by a prism spread of violet is most while least for red, (However, in grating spectra spread of violet is least while of red most).

3.As for a given prism $\theta \propto \delta $, a single prism produces both deviation and dispersion simultaneously, i.e., a single prism cannot give deviation without dispersion or dispersion without deviation.

However, if two prisms are combined together we can get deviation without dispersion or vice-versa. So if two prisms of prism angles A and ${A}'$ and refractive indices $\mu $ and ${\mu }'$

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~\delta ={{\delta }_{1}}+{{\delta }_{2}}=\left( \mu -1 \right)\text{A}+\left( {\mu }'-1 \right)\text{{A}' } \\ \text{and} & \theta ~={{\theta }_{1}}+{{\theta }_{2}}=\left( {{\mu }_{\text{V}}}-{{\mu }_{\text{R}}} \right)\text{A}+\left( \mu _{\text{V}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}-\mu _{\text{R}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }} \right)\text{{A}'} \\ \end{array}$

1.For deviation without dispersion 

$\theta =0\text{, }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{A}'=\frac{\left( {{\mu }_{\text{V}}}-{{\mu }_{R}} \right)}{\left( \mu _{\text{V}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}-\mu _{\text{R}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }} \right)}\text{A}$ …(16)

i.e., the two prisms must be placed with their prism angles in opposite directions so that condition given by Eq. (16) may be satisfied. In this situation

$\delta =\left( \mu -1 \right)A-\left( {\mu }'-1 \right)\frac{\left( {{\mu }_{\text{V}}}-{{\mu }_{\text{R}}} \right)}{\left( \mu _{\text{V}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}-\mu _{\text{R}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }} \right)}\text{A}$

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\delta =\left( \mu -1 \right)A\left[ 1-\frac{\left( {{\mu }_{\text{V}}}-{{\mu }_{\text{R}}} \right)}{\left( \mu -1 \right)}\times \frac{\left( {\mu }'-1 \right)}{\left( \mu _{\text{V}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}-\mu _{\text{R}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }} \right)} \right]$

But as $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\delta }_{1}}=\left( \mu -1 \right)\text{A},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\omega =\frac{\left( {{\mu }_{\text{V}}}-{{\mu }_{\text{R}}} \right)}{\left( \mu -1 \right)}\text{A}$

$\text{and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\omega }_{1}}=\frac{\left( \mu _{\text{V}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}-\mu _{\text{R}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }} \right)}{\left( {\mu }'-1 \right)},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\delta ={{\delta }_{1}}\left[ 1-\left( \omega /{\omega }' \right) \right]$ …(17)

usually ${\omega }'>\omega $, so $\delta $ is in the same direction as produced by the first prism [Fig. (A)].

The prism which produces deviation without dispersion is called 'achromatic prism'. 

2.For dispersion without deviation 

$\delta =0\text{, }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{A}'=\frac{\left( \mu -1 \right)}{\left( {\mu }'-1 \right)}A$ …(18)

In this situation,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \theta =\left( {{\mu }_{\text{V}}}-{{\mu }_{\text{R}}} \right)\text{A}-\left( \mu _{\text{V}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}-\mu _{\text{R}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }} \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\left( \mu -1 \right)}{\left( {\mu }'-1 \right)}\text{A} …(19) \\ {} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{i}\text{.e}\text{.} & \theta =\left( \mu -1 \right)A\left[ \frac{\left( {{\mu }_{\text{V}}}-{{\mu }_{\text{R}}} \right)}{\left( \mu -1 \right)}-\frac{\left( {\mu }'{{~}_{\text{V}}}-\mu _{\text{R}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }} \right)}{\left( {\mu }'-1 \right)} \right] \\ {} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{or} & \theta =\delta \left[ \omega -{\omega }' \right] \\ \end{array}$

If ${\omega }'>\omega $, the resultant dispersion is negative, i.e., opposite to that produced by the first prism. The prism which produces dispersion without deviation is called 'direct vision prism' .
